questions.scenario: A parser reserves 8 bytes for a payload but copies 12 bytes because it trusted a packet length field. Which risk is this?
questions.explanation: The write spills past the intended memory area, which is the shape of a buffer overrun.

questions.scenario: Code keeps an address-like handle to heap data, the owner cleans that data up, and later code reads through the old handle. Which risk is this?
questions.explanation: Use-after-free means code tries to use memory after the owner has released it.

questions.scenario: Two threads read and write the same value at the same time, and the result depends on timing. Which article term names this risk?
questions.explanation: A data race is unsynchronized shared access where at least one access mutates the data.

questions.scenario: Which statements match the article vocabulary?

options.text: A pointer is an address-like value that tells code where data lives.
A reference is Rusts safer way to access data without becoming its owner.
A String value owns growable text and points to heap bytes.
The heap is cleaned only when the operating system restarts.

questions.explanation: The first three statements match the article. Rust owned values are dropped at known program points.
